speaker: interviewer
text: Irene, she's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Irene--

speaker: interviewer
text: --a saint. She was unique.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, Irene was a goddamn angel. Sometimes I have to believe in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is rolling. We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling bars now. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: When they say, speed, talk about the Nazi--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're not. We're just rolling bars now. You can ID the tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. We're interviewing, on July 7, 1995, Magda Mautner Marx. This is tape four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four. Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then we arrived through the gate. There was a little light. So I looked who-- with whom I came there. It was a-- a quite good-looking, tall, blond, German soldier and his uncle, [? Mihai, ?] who was one of the leader in the party. He was looking at me. And-- and the-- and the German soldier looking.

speaker: interviewer
text: He knew my paper. He was-- in his hand were my papers. And I told-- he asked, who is-- who is this solider? I said, it is none of your business. Please open the gate. I want that he comes in for a cup of tea. And I told-- this I told, said in Hungarian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I told the soldier that he will let-- let you come in, because I invite you for a cup of tea. And he was very glad to come in. And there were this nurse. When he saw that I came in with a German soldier, I think that she fainted. [LAUGHS] She didn't know what to think now, who I am. And she didn't speak German. And the soldier didn't speak Hungarian. So I translated the conversation, what with me, you know, what she said. And I translated something else in German. Then what he said, I translated something else in Hungarian. So it was a nice conversation, though not one word was true. [LAUGHS] And so I told the nurse that I met this soldier quite a few days ago. And-- and we like each other. And if he doesn't mind, I invited him for a cup of tea. No, no, no. So they made a cup of tea. And there was this conversation. And then the-- then he left. And he asked me if I permit that he come some other time. I said, gladly, come whenever you want to, but whenever you can, too. So he was-- his name was, Joseph Reichel. He was an-- an airplane constructor. He lived in Germany, Karlsruhe, on Adlerstrasse 3. I know the address even. And then, next day, they came up with a jeep, with another one, Karl. And it was, maybe, twice he could come. And then he was called back. He-- he was a driver of a tank. He was called back. And then Karl, the friend, came, brought me letters from him. And such love letters, I-- never in my life, I read in German. And it was-- [BACKGROUND SOUND] started to be close to Christmas. And then once Karl came and said, now, they are going out. Not very far from there is a town, Ercsi. And there, the whole troop is. And the day before, there were very heavy fighting there. I thought, [SIGHS] maybe it would be a good idea to go. And if we-- maybe, the Russian come in, everybody will be hiding, because the fighting is everybody goes to some shelter. Maybe the-- if the Russian could win, I could go over. And I am over with this whole story. So I don't know how I could do all these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came with a small jeep. And there were-- in the back, there were big containers, food that they took to the front for the soldiers. And so I-- it was only two seats, so I was sitting in one of them lap.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I want to see Joseph. So I don't know what will happen, you know, so I want to see him. With this story, I went. And we went through Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: I go, my God, if somebody would see that I sit in a German jeep on a German [LAUGHS] soldier's lap? But I had some kerchief, because it was very cold. So nobody was on the streets so early morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we arrived. And unfortunately, it was a quiet day, was no fighting, nothing. So I had to come back with them. But all this, that the people see that a German soldier is coming and going and bringing to me, everybody started to be afraid of me. They didn't know. I looked like a spy, or I don't know what. So they-- they started to be afraid of me. Of course, the next day, when the soldier came the next morning, with a big apology, they brought back my papers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So this went on for a few days. And one day, we-- we were walking. And this soldier, Joseph-- we're sitting somewhere-- started to cry. But really cried that he was 17 years old when they took him, the German Army, and then took him to-- to Africa. And then they took him back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- he doesn't know what is with his family. He-- the parents didn't live anymore. But she-- he had a sister. And he-- he didn't have a life from 17. He was 27. 10 years, he didn't have any-- any-- any life. And he was crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: What town was he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What town in Germany was he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Karlsruhe. I-- I said--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Karlsruhe Adler-- Adlerstrasse 3. I know even the address. And so it was-- you wanted-- and I-- I'm thinking, we have lift-- left out something. Yeah, in the meantime, I was so sure of-- with his protection. He said-- he asked me if I would marry him when the war is over. I said, if my-- if my fiance wouldn't come back, because who knows in war, then I would. And so I became his fiance. So I went down with him to his where the people were, a few soldiers. He was not a-- he was an under-officer. I don't know what the-- what-- what the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rank?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Rank.

speaker: interviewee
text: --what rank is this. And even he had the two SS, so a tod-- nah, no. Not-- not SS. It was like a-- a-- a skull with two bones, totenkopf. I think they belonged to the SS, but he was not an SS. He was a regular guy, who drove this tank and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So it came close at Christmas. And I told him, listen, you are alone. I am alone. We have no family. It would be so beautiful if we would spend the Christmas together. And he said he-- I cannot. I-- how can I, if I am-- some wanted to drive the tank? I cannot. I said, if you are a technician, you could do something for a day. He said, what do you mean? I said, I don't mean anything. Think of it. So it was December, 20-- 24. In the meantime, I told him, because-- I told him, listen, everybody can see that the German lost this war. They are already here in Hungary. They lost this war. Why you don't give up? And it is the-- Germany will have much more use of you if you are alive than if you are dead. In-- in war can happen anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, if-- if he would know that the English come and not the Russians, maybe the whole group, they-- they talk about that. But not for the Russians, they wouldn't give up. And I told him, listen, know one thing. I will have here clothing, regular clothing, you know, like the-- not-- not uniform. And if you see that it is no-- no more any way, come here, and-- and I will hide you. And I really would do it, because I-- I thought he saved me. However, he didn't know that he saved me. But I know, and I would save him. But what happened in-- in the 24 of December, 1944, were very, very heavy fighting. And the people went on, didn't have a shelter. There was a szuterin, with big window on the outside. And all the sick people, everybody, had to go there. And we decided, with this nurse, that we don't want to go. Because it-- everybody's coughing and spitting. And it is quite impossible not to get the tuberculosis.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed in this little building. And suddenly came a Hungarian, here, all around with the bullets and in the hand-- and he was drunk-- and in the hand, a hand grenade.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came into the kitchen. And he played with  the hand grenade. Even though, if I would drop this, we will all go to the air.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I sat there, OK, that's very nice. Listen, here, you have some-- we've made a big something for Christmas. Here, you have some cake and, here, a drink, a glass of wine of all we have had and leave. So he did. And he left, luckily.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a few minutes later, I look out of the window, and I saw-- saw some funny soldier there. I saw the Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH], I don't know. This doesn't look like a German soldier. He goes around and look into the window. Take-- come, take a look.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a Russian soldier. It was a-- this Mongol-looking, this-- with the-- with a long, gray coat. Not even-- there was a-- there's a seam on it. He hanged-- hanged a little thing out of it and had this fur coat, with the here, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, this-- this is not a German solider. It's better if we go now to the shelter, even if it is tuberculosis. It's not-- not good if there are two women here. And we went down there. And I-- I tried to sit close to the window, in a corner, not between all the people. And then they-- the-- there were heavy fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one of these-- one of the nurses said, let's sitting around and-- and to make a 'Hail Mary' 20 times. I didn't know the 'Hail Mary,' but-- so I had to sit, also, and mumbled like with the others, you know? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, again, I felt I-- I have to take a bath. So I went up to the other-- to-- to the big building. This shelter was in the big building. And I just take a bath. It was a big boom.

speaker: interviewer
text: I jump out. When-- and I thought, I am still alive, why should I run? So I went down. And where I was sitting, usually, there came not a bomb but, you know, a cannon-- I don't know you call it-- ball and killed two people there. And aI was up in the attic. Nothing happened to me. [BACKGROUND SOUND] So it was maybe passed two days, and then come one of these Polish men. And he sit down next to me, and he said, you know I am Jewish? I said, OK, but why do you say this to me? Because we know that you are Jewish. But you played your role quite well. And now, we go down to the prefector, because we understand Russian. And we decide that-- that we volunteer to be-- to be translators. And you know, still, we cannot know what happens. Would you-- would you come and not to stay here? Because it can-- in the war can happen that, for one day, they take back the same place, and then you are lost. Because-- It's better if you come. I said, OK, I-- I-- I-- I go. I go where the Russian goes. So we went down. And we didn't know, not he and not me, that it was a curfew. This was already Russian. And so this Joseph-- yeah, I-- I left out a very important thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in the 23rd evening, suddenly Joseph came. He was-- the-- the eyes were red, and he was like if you would be drunk. But he was not drunk. He said, he was three days in the tank. And he is that tired. And he-- he said, I did what you told me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I though now, I am in a trouble, you know, because I don't know what he wants, you know, to spend with me the night or what-- what. But he was so tired that he fell on bed like dead. I was sitting all night in the kitchen. And I didn't know. Again, I had a problem, you know? So, what should I do with him? But in 4 o'clock at night, banging on the door and came Karl. Is Joseph here? I said, yes. He's sleeping. He has to come, because we move. He couldn't wake him up. So he dragged him to the jeep and took him away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now-- so now I go back. I went down with this-- with this Polish man. And the patrol caught up. It is curfew when-- it is 7:00 or 8 o'clock. You're not supposed to be on the street. And he explained, in Russian, that we didn't know about the curfew. So he took this man away. And they put me in a-- with somebody in an empty kitchen and locked me in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, he-- spy, German spy, spy, I-- I understand, at least this word, what he said, for both of us, that we are spies.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they took him away. And me, they put in a-- locked in, in a kitchen. And I had, with me, all these letters, what this soldier wrote to me. So I thought, what should I do if they start to search me, and they find the-- the letter from a German soldier, and I-- I look like, really, a spy? So I-- they-- they throw me a few a-- a straw in. And-- so I found, in this kitchen, a-- a match. So I put in the stove the letters and the straw. Anyhow, it was very cold. But now, I am sorry, because I could prove it, you know? But I couldn't risk my life for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, start-- I don't know if you want to hear what happened after that, because this was-- I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: We want to know--

speaker: interviewee
text: --three times in-- in Russian-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, how would I even? Like they-- they capture people, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: A center.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sort of prison, yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: A prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They imprisoned you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three different times?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three different times--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: --they captured me. In Budakeszi.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Budakeszi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was in Budakeszi.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same town.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same town. And the second time-- the second time was not in-- the third time was not in Budakeszi.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you finally escape from the Russian problem, where they imprisoned you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, again, a very long story, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, just give us the idea.

words.text: Well, just give us the idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I didn't have where there I have-- where I am, what-- what should I do. No, I couldn't use any more of my-- [LAUGHS] my-- my Christian paper. So I, the next morning, the 'davai' let me out. And there was a big group of-- of people in-- in their hospital uniform in February. No, this was not February. It was in December. But it was very cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- they-- some had these gray little blanket on them in-- in-- in slippers. And I asked them-- they were Hungarians-- what happened to you? They were in a hospital, and somebody shoot to the-- to the Russians. And they-- a whole, a whole-- Evacuated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Evacuated.

speaker: interviewer
text: The hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole-- not the whole hospital, only this room, what they at-- they suspected that somebody shoot. And were-- were no women only men. And there came the soldier and opened and 'davai,' go with-- I had to go with them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did-- [LAUGHS] I was not very happy about that, of course. So-- and I was cold. So we went, went, went. And then I stopped. And I said, I don't go. No. I am cold. I am hungry. And I am cold. I don't go. So but really, I didn't know why they kept me. I was the only woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- later, I realized that they have to have a certain number. They captured 200 or 300 prisoners. And if I-- let me go, one is missing. So I had to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now they didn't want that I go with all these men, so I had to go on the side. And here, there were the Russian soldiers, with the weapon. [BACKROUND NOISE] So I couldn't-- I couldn't escape. And then in one spot, I decide that-- I told them, I don't go. And I stopped. The whole group had to stop. And it was a little-- a house or something, you know, it-- it-- you know, on the railroad station. And they took me. One took me in. And they told the woman that to give me something to eat. There I got some half-cooked meal. And so they went. And two hours later, I said, again, I don't go, because I am cold. [LAUGHS] I don't know why I did that. I have to. This part was very terrible. But-- but I don't know why I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they let me go in. There were a lot of empty houses, these [NON-ENGLISH] run away from the Russians. And I found a long gown, something flannel gown. So I took this and took under-- under-- on me and on my head. And then I walked again. So we walked three days. In the meantime, I saw that they catch other people. I think somebody could escape. I couldn't escape, because I had to go not in the group but on the side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we arrived to a town. Eat-- they didn't eat, not the-- the Russians either. They didn't eat all day. They arrived some-- at some town about 4 o'clock. And then they took the whole group in a barn. They cooked something. And me, they took they-- they always to the owner of the house to watch out of me that I shouldn't escape. And they-- they give me something to eat. And I-- don't ask. This was-- I-- I was sleeping in a box what was full with fleas and on-- on a chair. And it was-- was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on the third day-- there was one man who could speak Russian. And they used him as a translator. The third day, we arrived to Ersci. They took-- went into the prefector. I suppose they had already the 300 people. And they thought that these men and I, we can go. So I was in a strange place. I never was there in my life. So I decided I go back to Budakeszi. So I went back and met a woman on the street. And I saw that-- you-- you-- we recognized somehow if somebody is Jewish. And I ask her, are you Jewish? Yes. We-- we found here a room. I told, I-- I have nowhere to go. And he said, we have a room. The half is full. We put down straw. And we sleep on this straw. And you can have a-- a-- a corner also to sleep with us. So I-- I accepted. I was very glad, first of all, that my people was-- the lady was the mother of Andras Korodi. He was a famous-- famous conductor in the Hungarian opera house. And the other, they had a- a salon, a dress salon. It was a couple and a 16-years-old girl and 13-years-old boy. So we were together. Now we had nothing to eat. I went to the prefector. I had a little-- a-- a little card, what let me use the sanatorium's vehicle to go to town. So they-- they don't know these Latin letters, so I showed sanatorium, and I am a nurse, I said. And the-- this was my-- Pass.

speaker: interviewee
text: --pass. So, OK, they need somebody. And they have a room. And I have to hire some people who will clean the room. And I have to serve them food. And I have to-- my job will be to sterilize little bottles, which I don't know what was in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, OK. This I can do, you know. They told me, they have-- they have to put in it something and let it boil 10 minutes. And that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this solved our whole family problem. Because I told them, you will be the cleaning woman. I-- [LAUGHS] I am the nurse. And we will get food for it. And we-- every day, we got a big-- a big pot of Russian soup, what is everything in it, cabbage and potato and onion and whatever you can image. And the evening, they had-- they call it cutlet, some-- like a hamburger kind of things. So they-- with a-- with a big-- big plate and some potato. And they eat one. And then they bite in another one. So what left, I put in a plate, and this was our dinner. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
